coredata.dc:title: Novel fluorescent hydrogel for the adsorption and detection of Fe (III)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Dongdong
Tian, Xuetao
Li, Huanhuan
Zhao, Yiping
Chen, Li
coredata.dc:description: A novel PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with good adsorption property and stable fluorescence detection for Fe3+ was prepared by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. As a fluorescence probe, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were modified by glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to obtain PCDs, which had double bond and could react with N-isopropyl acrylamide to form hydrogel. The functionalization of the amine groups on the surface of the PCDs enables PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel also exhibits photoluminescence behavior. In addition, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels stably shows higher adsorption performance for Fe3+ than the pure PNIPAAm hydrogel. The honeycomb PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel had a good quenching response to metallic iron ions, and the detection limit was as low as 0.27 μM in the range of 1μM～1mM. The results of adsorption isotherms and adsorption kinetics showed that the adsorption procedure of metal ions conforms to the pseudo-second-order model and langmuir model, respectively. The effective detection and the high adsorption of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel for Fe3+ make it an effective portable platform potential, which has the dual functions of visual detection and adsorption of Fe3+.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrogel
Carbon dots
Fluorescent
Heavy metal ions
Ions detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PNIPAAm-CD and PNIPAAm hydrogels

IRT-17R80 ) and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (grant number. TD13-5044 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of PCDs and PNIPAAm-CDs hydrogel.
(a) TEM image, (b) XPS survey spectrum, (c) C1s core-level XPS spectra and (d) N1s core-level XPS spectra of CDs. Inset images of (a) are size distribution histogram of CDs.

(a) FTIR spectrum of CDs, PCDs, PNIPAAm hydrogel and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel; (b) Mechanical measurement of PNIPAAm hydrogel and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel; Surface morphologies of (c) PNIPAAm hydrogel and (d) PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel.
(a) DSC thermograms, (b) TGA and (c) DTG curves of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels.

(a) CDs detection of metal ions, (b) combination of CDs and Fe3+, (c) UV/Vis absorption and PL emission spectra (λex = 370 nm) of PCDs in aqueous solution, (d) PL emission spectra of the PNIPAAM-CD hydrogel and inset of the normalized PL emission spectra, (e) photographs of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels before and after addition of Fe3+ under Day light and UV light, (f) Fluorescent response of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel to Fe3+ (λex = 385 nm).

(a)The equilibrium adsorption amount of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels to Fe3+; (b) pseudo-first order and (c) pseudo-second order kinetics plots for the adsorption of the Fe3+ on PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels.
(a) Effect of initial concentrate of Fe3+ on adsorption; (b) linear plots of Langmuir and (c) freundlich isotherms for Fe3+ adsorption by PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels.
Schematic illustration for the binding of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with Fe3+.
Comparison of the detection and adsorption of Fe3+ ions using different Materials.

row.entry: CdTe quantum dots doped hydrogel optical fiber

head.ce:title: Novel fluorescent hydrogel for the adsorption and detection of Fe (III)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China National Pulp and Paper Research Institute Co., Ltd. Sinolight Plaza, 4 Qiyang Road, Chaoyang, Beijing 100102, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China National Pulp and Paper Research Institute Co., Ltd. Sinolight Plaza

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China National Pulp and Paper Research Institute Co., Ltd. Sinolight Plaza, 4 Qiyang Road, Chaoyang, Beijing 100102, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Tianjin Polytechnic University, Tianjin 300387, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with good adsorption property and stable fluorescence detection for Fe3+ was prepared by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. As a fluorescence probe, carbon quantum dots (CDs) were modified by glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to obtain PCDs, which had double bond and could react with N-isopropyl acrylamide to form hydrogel. The functionalization of the amine groups on the surface of the PCDs enables PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel also exhibits photoluminescence behavior. In addition, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels stably shows higher adsorption performance for Fe3+ than the pure PNIPAAm hydrogel. The honeycomb PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel had a good quenching response to metallic iron ions, and the detection limit was as low as 0.27 μM in the range of 1μM～1mM. The results of adsorption isotherms and adsorption kinetics showed that the adsorption procedure of metal ions conforms to the pseudo-second-order model and langmuir model, respectively. The effective detection and the high adsorption of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel for Fe3+ make it an effective portable platform potential, which has the dual functions of visual detection and adsorption of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions have been a global problem to harm the ecological cycle and human public health [1], and could cause numerous diseases by accumulating in organisms [2]. Among them, Fe3+ is very common in all industrial wastewater with high toxicity, and its accumulation in the human body can cause vomiting, diarrhea and intestinal damage. Therefore, the detection and removal of Fe3+ has attracted the attention of many researchers [3]. Currently, many materials have been proposed to remove heavy metal ions from the environment, such as magnetic nanoparticles, membranes, and hydrogels [4–6]. Among the various removal methods of Fe3+, some smart materials have attracted people's attention due to many functional groups, stimulus response, low cost, high efficiency, and environmental friendliness [7]. It is worth noting that these new materials can only be used to adsorb metal ions, but cannot detect ions in solution. In order to efficiently detect Fe3+, fluorescent probe have attracted extensive attention because of their fluorescence properties [8]. For the detection of heavy metal ions, purely aliphatic materials are more acceptable than the conventional aromatic fluorescent materials because of their less susceptible to photodegadation and aging when they produce fluorescence [9,10]. Manas et.al [11] designed and prepared nonconventional multi-functional aliphatic terpolymers with high performance for remove Cu(II).

As a new type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon dots(CDs) have developed rapidly in recent years in the detection of heavy metal ions, biosensors [12], drug delivery and biological imaging [13], and so on, ascribing to its biocompatibility, low toxicity and fluorescence properties [8]. The CDs which was synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method from citric acid and ethylenediamine was proved suitable for environmental and biomedical applications due to the harmless to the human. One of the most promising applications of CDs is for the heavy metal ions fluorescent detection [14]. Lu et.al [15] designed a nitrogen-doped multifunctional CDs probe that detects the presence of Fe3+ ions through two-photon excitation.

CDs can be used as probes to directly detect metal ions in the environmental sewage, which is not conducive fast on-site identification for the timely fluorescence measurement in practical application [16]. The organic solution present in the actual wastewater makes the hydrophilic CDs easy to aggregate, resulting in its self-absorption and weakened PL intensity [17]. Most hydrophilic CDs are soluble in water and difficult to remove from the water, causing secondary pollution. In order to overcome these problems, there must be an appropriate supporter to achieve CDs dispersion and fixation [18].

Among numerous adsorption materials, the hydrogels have received increasing attention in terms of heavy metal removal due to their porous three-dimensional polymer network structure, easy loading of metal ions, reusability and large swelling ability. Recently, Gogoi [19] have prepared chitosan based hybrid CDs hydrogel membrane, which was used as solid response platform detecting heavy metal ions by hydrogen bonding. It has showed the capability of optical detecting to the heavy metal ions, and it also has a certain ability to remove these metal ions. The hybrid CDs hydrogel membrane shows the optical detection ability for heavy metal ions, and also has the ability to remove these metal ions. Generally, the CDs in the hybrid CDs hydrogel binds to the carrier through weak interaction, which is unstable in the water system. Once the prepared CDs hydrogel is put into water, it will cause secondary pollution due to the shedding of CDs. Therefore, it is necessary to prepare a suitable new material, which can make CDs exist stably in the hydrogel through covalent bond interaction. Moreover, modifiers that are covalently bonded to the surface of the substrate also have longer chemical stability than physical blending.

In this work, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with good adsorption property and stable fluorescence detection for Fe3+ was prepared by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. The effect PCDs on the structure, surface composition and thermosensitive properties of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel were investigated. The fluorescence quenching effect of PCDs at 370 nm with different metal ions (K+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ca2+ and Hg2+) was investigated. The results showed that Fe3+ can efficiently quench the fluorescence of PCDs. The results showed that Fe3+ can efficient quench the fluorescence of PCDs. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on the prepared CDs, PCDs and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel was investigated. In addition, the adsorption kinetics modules and isotherm models of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel were studied. All of the results showed that PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel has potential applications as an effective, portable platform with bifunctional of visual detection and adsorption of Fe3+.

1, 2-Ethylenediamine (EDA), Citric acid monohydrate, glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), dimethylformamide (DMF), ethanol (EtOH) and acetone were provided from Kermel. Quinine sulfate (98 %) was obtained from Aladdin. N,N,N',N'-tetramethylenediamine (TEMED), ammonium persulfate (APS), and N, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA) were purchased by Aldrich. N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAAm) was recrystallized from a 50:50 cyclohexane–toluene mixture before use. Fe3+ stock solution was obtained from chlorate salts. Unless stated otherwise, all reagents with analytical grade were used directly without further treatment and deionized water was used for the solution throughout all the experiments.

CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method according to Zhu and Yan’s method [20,21]. Typically, 536 μL ethylenediamine and 0.46 g citric acid monohydrate were added into 10 mL of deionized water. After the solid particles were completely dissolved, the resulting solution was poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated at 200℃ and held for 5 h [22]. The pH of the mixture naturally cooled to ambient temperature was slowly adjusted to 14 with a 1 M NaOH solution, and the color of the mixture solution became transparent brown-black. The solvent in this solution was removed by rotary evaporation to obtain a crude solid product. The obtained product was further dispersed in ethanol, and insoluble impurities were removed by centrifugation to obtain CDs supernatant. The ethanol was removed by evaporation to obtain a powder of solid CDs, which was further re-dissolved in deionized water. After being frozen at 4℃ for 24 h, the solution was filtered with a filter (0.22 μm) to remove aggregated particles. The final product was dried to obtain the CDs. The preparation of the PCDs was carried out by further functionalizing the CDs with GMA (Fig. 1 a), and the more detailed information was presented in ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PNIPAAm-CD and PNIPAAm hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with a three-dimensional structure was prepared by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. 0.65 g NIPAAm and 0.075 g PCDs were dissolved in 4.25 mL water containing 0.025 g MBAA and the resulting dispersion was cooled to 0 °C. After being kept under nitrogen for 20 min, the solution was added to the initiator APS (0.020 g) and the accelerator TEMED (20 μL) in sequence at 5 min intervals. The mixed solution was sealed and stored at room temperature for 2 days, and the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel shown in Fig. 1 was obtained. As a control, a pure PNIPAAm hydrogel without PCDs was also synthesized. Before being tested, all prepared hydrogels were immersed in water and washed with deionized water and stored in water.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR, TENSOR37) spectrometer was employed to record surface groups of hydrogels and the PCDs surface elements were determinate by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EDAX GENESIS 60S). The images of CDs were obtained with a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI H-7650), and the TEM sample was prepared by dropping a dispersion solution of PCDs on a carbon membrane supported by a 200-mesh copper grid. The SEM images of hydrogel were obtained with a Hitachi S4800 electronic microscope. XRD profile of the PCDs was recorded on a Bruker AXS D8 DISCOVER diffractometer equipped with CuKα (λ = 1.54 Å) radiation in the range from 10° to 70°. 13C-NMR and 1H-NMR curves of the samples were measured with a Bruke AVANCE AV-300 MHz NMR spectrometer. DSC measurement was performed with a PERKIN ELMER DSC7 differential scanning calorimeter. The thermo degradation of the hydrogel were investigated by TG (STA409PC) and DTG. Compressive strength was measured by Electromechanical Universal Testing Machine (CMT 4503) at a rate of 10 mm/min. The volume of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels subjected to uniaxial compression is 25 × 25 × 25 mm3.

The fluorescence imaging of the CDs (50 ug/mL) was obtained by ZF-20D black-box-type ultraviolet analyzer under 360 nm. The fluorescence photographs of the hydrogels were observed at UV, blue and green light excitations under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Ti-U). The photoluminescence curves were collected on an F-380 fluorescence spectrophotometer under the 350−390 nm excitation wavelength with the emission slit width of 5 nm. The hydrogel of size 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 and thickness 0.1 cm were added into 2 mL metal solutions with various concentrations at room temperature under excitation wavelength of 385 nm. UV–vis absorption spectra were taken from a UV–vis spectrometer (Purkinje General TU-1901). The lifetime of CDs, PCDs and they mixed with Fe3+ were collected on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments).

The adsorption properties of hydrogels were investigated with ICP-AES (Model: Varian 715-ES, US). A hydrogel with a size of 0.5 × 0.5 cm and a thickness of 0.1 cm was placed in 25 mL of 200 mg L−1 metal solution. After standing for 1 day, the concentration of metal ions in the solution before and after adsorption was analyzed by ICP-AES. The amount of adsorption q (mg/g) of hydrogels were evaluated as:
(1)
q =
C 0
-
C f
V
/ m
Where C0 and Cf (mg/L) are the actual concentrations of metal ions in the solution before and after adsorption by the hydrogels samples; V (L) is the volume of the whole system solution; m (g) is the weight of the original dry hydrogels used to test.

The fabrication diagram of the new PNIPAAm-CDs hydrogel combining modified CDs and responsive polymer is shown in Fig. 1. By mixing citric acid and ethylenediamine, the CDs with uniform size and high fluorescence quantum yield are prepared by hydrothermal method. Due to the presence of an epoxy ring that can interact with ethylenediamine and a carbon-carbon double bond that can react with NIPAAm in the side chain, glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) was selected as the surfactant to further modify the obtained ODs. After being synthesized by hydrothermal method, CDs were further functionalized with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to produce polymerizable carbon dots (PCDs). Considering that the prepared gel needs to have strong adsorption capacity, N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAAm) was used as the original monomer for the preparation of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel. By copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomer to prepare PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel, which can disperse and fix the PCDs through strong chemical bonds to avoid PCDs leakage in water.

The morphology and surface elements of CDs in water were displayed in Fig. 2 . From the morphology of the CDs measured by TEM, it can be seen that the prepared CDs are spherical shapes with the average size of 7 nm in diameter (insets in Fig. 2a). And there was no lattice fringe in the images, indicating that the obtained CDs had an amorphous nature. This is consistent with the XRD result, which showed an obvious broad diffraction peak at 22° (Fig. S1) [23]. The small size of the amorphous structure may give PCDs unique fluorescent properties, which will be discussed later. In the wide-scan XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2b), there are three major emission peaks at 285.37 eV, 400.13 eV and 532.09 eV, suggesting the existence of C1s, N1s and O1s [24]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2c) have three constitute of carbon bonds, corresponding to CC at 284.88 eV, CN at 286.48 eV, and CO at 288.18 eV, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2d, the deconvoluted N1s spectra can be curve fitted with three peak components at 399.2 eV, 400.1 eV and 400.6 eV, belonging to CNC, OCNH and NH, respectively [25], indicating the PCDs have been functionalized with glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) successfully.

The chemical compositions of CDs, PCDs, PNIPAAm hydrogel and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel were characterized by FTIR and the resulted spectra were plotted in Fig. 3 a. Compared with CDs, the spectra of the PCDs show two peaks around 1560 and 1714 cm−1, which can be attributed to the CO stretching and the −COOR stretching bands. These functional groups confirmed that GMA was introduced to the surface of CDs successfully, which made it convenient for the CDs’ further applications. The main infrared characteristic peaks are collected in the Tab. S1. With reference to quinine sulfate, the quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 45 % at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. S2). Moreover, the functional groups on the surface of PCDs and CDs were further conformed by 1H-NMR (Fig. S3) and 13C-NMR spectrum (Fig. S4). The main 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectrum characteristic peaks were collected in Tab. S1 and Tab. S2. In the PNIPAAm-CD spectrum, not only the CO characteristic absorption peaks of CDs can be observed, but also the positions of the NH, OH and CH tensile vibration absorption peaks in the spectrum are the same as PNIPAAm. In addition, the spectra of PNIPAAm-CD showed NH bending vibration peak of the amide group, vibration peaks of COC or CO at 1560 cm−1, 1170 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, respectively, suggesting the successful preparation of PNIPAAm-CD [26].

Mechanical measurements were performed to detect the effect of addition of PCDs on the mechanical strength of the hydrogel, and the results are shown in the Fig. 3b. Using a uniaxial universal compressor to apply pressure to hydrogels to compress them. When the height of the hydrogel is compressed from 2.5 cm to 1.25 cm (that is, 50 % deformation), the pressure is about 115 K Pa. After removing the pressure, the hydrogel can be completely recovered, indicating that both PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels have good elasticity. Continue to increase the pressure, PNIPAAM hydrogel produced irreparable cracks when the deformation reached 60 %. The PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel will produce the same cracks when the deformation reaches 70 %, which shows that the addition of PCDs has enhanced the mechanical strength of PNIPAAm hydrogel to a certain extent.

Fig. 3c shows that the PNIPAAm hydrogels have a honeycomb structure. Compared with PNIPAAm hydrogel, PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel has a more regular pore structure and a larger surface area (Fig. 3d). This may be due to PCDs increasing the cross-linking point of the gel and hindering the formation of large pores, resulting in the appearance of such relatively small pore structure (tens to hundreds of nanometers) [27].The more regular pore structure of PNIPAAm-CD provides the hydrogel with more energy to consume pressure, making the hydrogel have better compressive strength. And the formation of this structure is more conducive to the adsorption of harmful metal ions. In addition, no aggregation of PCDs was observed in the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel, indicating that the distribution of PCDs on the surface of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel was relatively uniform. In order to determine the structural stability, recyclability, and flexibility of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel, repeated swelling and deswelling were conducted [28,29]. As shown in Fig. S9, the swelling and deswelling rate were calculated to be 2.44 (g/g) and 58.26 %, respectively.

As a typical thermo-sensitive polymer, PNIPAAm can exhibit a unique conformational transition near a low critical solution temperature (LCST) [30]. Considering these promising properties of PNIPAAm, the thermosensitive properties of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel were studied. As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the volume phase transition temperature (VPTT) value and endothermic peak of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is lower compared with PNIPAAm, which may be due to the introduction of hydrophilic groups, such as the amino and hydroxyl groups of PCD, in PNIPAAm-CD. This may interfere with the initial hydrophilic-hydrophobic balance of the PNIPAAm molecular chain, which in turn interferes with the hydrogen bond-group enhancement between water molecules and –CO-NH. Therefore, PNIPAAm-CD needs more energy than PNIPAAm to break the newly added hydrogen bonds. In addition, after the combination of PCD and PNIPAAm, the LCST of the hydrogel will increase slightly.

Fig. 4(b) and (c) show that the weight loss of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels mainly occurs in two stages from room temperature to 700 °C. The first stage of weight loss is mainly the loss of bound water in the gel between 80−200 °C, and the water specific gravity of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is about 10 % of the total weight. The second stage of weightlessness mainly occurs between 300−450 °C due to decarboxylation, chain scission and depolymerization of the deaminated structure, and the final product is less than 1% of the gel weight [31]. The maximum weight loss rate of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel at 413.3 °C, which is slightly higher than the temperature at which PNIPAAm hydrogel has significant weight loss. This may be due to the increased crosslink density of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel. Thus, it can be concluded that the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is relatively stable at 100 °C, which is very beneficial to the application of the hydrogel in actual wastewater treatment.

By testing the fluorescence intensity of CDs and PCDs at different excitation wavelengths (350 nm–390 nm), it was found that the best fluorescence intensity was around 448 nm at the excitation wavelength of 370 nm, as shown in Fig. S5a and S5b. After adding 2 mL of Fe3+ solution (3.6 mmol/L) into 50 ug/mL of CDs or PCDs aqueous solution, the fluorescence quenching effect of CDs at 370 nm with different metal ions (K+, Ni2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Fe2+,Co2+,Ca2+ and Hg2+) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), except that Fe3+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs, most metal ions have no effect on the PL emission of CDs. Therefore, CDs can be used to specifically detect Fe3+ ions. According to Yang's report [32], the use of CDs for the detection of Fe3+ is based on the coordination of Fe3+ with the -COOH, −OH, -NH2 and amide groups of CDs or accompanied by electron transfer, leading to the reduction of fluorescence. The interaction between CDs and Fe3+ is shown in Fig. 5(b), and the quenching mechanism will be described in detail later. In addition, it also can be found from Fig. 5a that Cu2+ and Hg2+ can quench CDs. This may due to the Cu2+ or Hg2+ complex with multi-amine groups of ethylenediamine could strongly destroy the supramolecular interaction between CDs and amine groups, leading to the inner filter effect of complex of Cu2+ or Hg2+ with amine groups [33].

From the UV/Vis spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 5c), two peaks around 240 nm and 344 nm can be observed. Among them, the peak near 240 nm is regarded as the absorption peak of the carbon core itself, which corresponds to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains. Another absorption peak at 344 nm is attributed to the absorption peak of the CDs surface state or molecular state, indicating the n-π* transition of -NH2 [34]. Upon excitation at 370 nm, the fluorescence spectrum of the aqueous CDs exhibits a distinct absorption peak at about 448 nm with a strong blue emission, which confirmed that the CDs has a larger Stokes shift, that is, the fluorescence spectrum of the CDs has a larger red shift than the corresponding absorption spectrum.

From the photographs of the aqueous CDs solution under sunlight and ultraviolet light (inset in Fig. 5c), it can be seen that the CDs is well dispersed in water. More importantly, the aqueous solution of CDs exhibit strong blue fluorescence in aqueous solution under UV light, which may be caused by the carbon surface of numerous functional groups [35]. As shown in inset of Fig. 5(c), the blue fluorescent photos of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel were recorded at UV excitation light. Under UV excitation light, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel showed blue fluorescence, indicating that the CDs could show excitation-dependent PL behavior even if them were copolymerized into the PNIPAAm polymer, it could show excitation-dependent PL behavior. To further explore the excitation-dependent PL behavior of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels, detailed PL characterization was performed at different excitation wavelengths from 350 to 390 nm. As shown in Fig. 5d, a higher fluorescence intensity can be observed in the emission spectrum of NIPAAm-CD under excitation at 385 nm. As the excitation wavelength increases, the fluorescence maximum of NIPAAm-CD gradually shifts to red, which may be caused by the functionalization of amine groups on the surface of PCDs. The lone-pair electron in the amino nitrogen increases the electron density and decreases the energy gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the CDs surface domain, thereby shifting the fluorescence maximum to a higher position [36].

Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on the prepared CDs and hydrogel was studied by observing the change of fluorescence intensity before and after adding Fe3+ under sunlight and ultraviolet light. As shown in Fig. 5e, the introduction of Fe3+ significantly reduced the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which is consistent with the test results of Fig. 5a. However, the fluorescence intensity of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel was weakened after the addition of Fe3+. The reason is that the luminescent CDs on the surface of the hydrogel is quenched when it comes into contact with Fe3+, while the internal CDs still fluoresces under ultraviolet light. Comparing the UV–vis spectra of CDs-Fe3+ and PCDs-Fe3+ in Fig. S5c and 5d, it was found that the addition of Fe3+ significantly increases the intensity of the UV absorption peak [10,37]. In order to study the effect of solvent polarity on fluorescence performance, fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectra of CDs in H2O, DMF, EtOH, and acetone were collected (Fig. S6). The fluorescence intensity of CDs was observed all gradually decreased with time in acetone, DMF, H2O and EtOH, indicating that the solvent has no effect on the absorption and emission spectra of CDs.

The stability of CDS and the detection process of Fe3+ were shown in Fig. S7. After combining with Fe3+ (Fig. S8), the intensity of the life test curve of CDs or PCDs is reduced, and an extra peak appears on the curve. To further investigate whether Fe3+ ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel, the sensitivity, linear response range and detection limit of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel sensing system were measured under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 5(f), the fluorescence intensity of the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is sensitive to Fe3+ and decreases with increasing Fe3+ concentration. It is worth noting that the paramagnetism of Fe3+ is also a reason for reducing the fluorescence intensity of CDs [38]. This relationship is described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(2) F0/F = 1+KSV [Q]
where F0 and F are the photon flux of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively, [Q] is the concentrations of Fe3+ and KSV represents the Stern-Volmer constant [39].

The calibration curve in the inset of Fig. 5(f) shows that F0/F-1 and Fe3+ concentration have a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9969) in the range of 1 μM to 1 mM, implying a static quenching [3]. According to calculations by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the detection limit (LOD) of Fe3+ is 0.27 μM, which is far below the maximum Fe3+concentration in drinking water of 5.35 μM. These results indicate that the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with strong FL activity has broad application prospects in the detection of Fe3+.

Fig. 6 a showed the measured adsorption capacities for Fe3+ of different hydrogels, which is according to the ion fluorescent detection analysis. Compared with PNIPAAm hydrogel (58.02 mg g−1), PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel has a higher adsorption capacity for Fe3+ (208.2 mg g−1), which shows that the addition of PCDs has a profound effect on the adsorption capacity of hydrogel. This is because the addition of PCDs increases the crosslinking point of the polymer, allowing more functional groups to chelate and absorb Fe3+. In addition, a large number of functional groups on the surface of PCDs can also serve as ligands for chelating Fe3+, making Fe3+ have a strong affinity for the functional groups of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel. Thus, it can be said that the prepared PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is expected to become a bifunctional material that can qualitatively detect and remove harmful metal ions.

The actual adsorption experiment results of PNIPAAm and PNIPAAm-CD hydrogels to Fe3+ showed that the adsorption process of hydrogels on Fe3+ reaches equilibrium for about 20 min, which will improve the removal efficiency of metal ions from water. In addition, the adsorption kinetics modules of the hydrogel were studied to evaluate their estimated contact time required to reach adsorption equilibrium. The results are shown in Fig. 6b and 6c. Generally, the adsorption behavior was described with adsorption rate using pseudo-primary and pseudo-secondary mechanisms [40], and the two kinetic rate equations were shown in (3) and (4):

Pseudo-first-order:
(3)
ln
(
q e
-
q t
)
=
ln
q e
-
k 1
t

Pseudo-second-order:
(4)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q e 2
+
t
q e
Where qe (mg/g) is the mass of adsorbed element per unit mass of samples prepared as adsorbent at equilibrium, qt (mg/g) is the adsorbed amount at time t, k1 (min−1) and k2 (g/mg·min−1) are equilibrium rate parameter of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-secondary adsorption interactions.

The linear and nonlinear correlation coefficients calculated by the common pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order adsorption models calculated using the regression program are shown in Tab. S4. After comparison, it was found that the correlation coefficient calculated by the quasi-second-order kinetic model is closer to the unit, thus deducing that the adsorption control mechanism of PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel for Fe3+ belongs to chemical adsorption.

The effect of initial concentrate of Fe3+ on the adsorption capacity of hydrogel were then investigated in depth and the results were shown in Fig. 7 a. In the concentrated solutions of Fe3+ with different concentrations, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel showed a higher adsorption capacity than the PNIPAAm hydrogel. Currently, different isotherm models including Langmuir and Freundlich models have been installed to study the adsorption isotherms of PNIPAm-CD hydrogels on PNIPA (Fig. 7b and c). Among them, the Langmuir isotherm model is considered to be single molecule adsorption, assuming uniform adsorption on the solid surface [41]. And the Langmuir isotherm is described as Eq. (5):
(5)
C e
q e
=
1
q m
b
+
C e
q m
Where Ce is the concentration (mg/L) of Fe3+ after equilibrium, qe is the Fe3+ mass adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g), qm is the adsorption amount of hydrogel(mg/g), and b is a relate constant (L/mg).

In addition, the Langmuir constant b was used to calculate the feasibility of the adsorption process, which was generally calculated by a dimensionless constant called the separation factor (RL), and this constant is represented by the formula (6):
(6)
R L
=
1
b
c o
+ 1

As an empirical model, the Freundlich isotherm contains heterogeneous adsorptive energies on the absorbent surface [42], which can be described by Eq. (7):
(7)
ln
q e
=
ln
K F
+
1 n
ln
c e
Where qe is the mass of Fe3+ adsorbed by hydrogels at equilibrium, ce in equation is the adsorbate (Fe3+) concentration at equilibrium, KF and n are parameters of the Freundlich model.

It can be seen from the relationship values in Tab. S5 that the Langmuir adsorption isotherms model is more acceptable for the adsorption isotherm of Fe3+, which indicates that the adsorption of Fe3+ by PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is limited by single layer coverage. And the value of the separation coefficient (RL) is in the range of 0 < 0.262 < 1, implying that the method is applicable. In conclusion, the adsorption of Fe3+ on the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel is not only the chelation of the functional groups in the hydrogel with Fe3+, but also the embedding of Fe3+ in the hydrogel. From the comparison of different materials for Fe3+detection and removal published in Table 1 , it is shown that our PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel has the previously reported materials with better detection and removal capabilities.

At present, there were four main models of fluorescence quench: fluorescence turn-on, fluorescence turn-off, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and ratiometric response [48]. Most of the CDs-based nano sensors were fluorescent turn-off, but the mechanism by which metal ions induce the actual fluorescence response has not been fully elucidated. It was deduced to the mechanism that Fe3+ ion quenched fluorescence the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel. On the one hand, the amino group on the surface of the CDs selectively responds to Fe3+ by a charge-off mechanism that is blocked by charge transfer and exciton recombination (Fig. 8 a). Another reason for fluorescence quenching is the internal filtering effect caused by the overlap between the absorption spectrum between PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel and the fluorescence emission spectrum or excitation spectrum of CD. Compared with other mechanisms, the internal filtering effect can increase the sensitivity, because the absorbance of the sensor will change exponentially with the change of the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8b).

In summary, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel with a three-dimensional structure was successfully synthesized by copolymerizing PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. The PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel can disperse and immobilize CDs through the strong chemical bond, which avoid leaked out under water. Therefore, the hydrogels were stable and showed the higher adsorption property for Fe3+ than the pure PNIPAAm hydrogel. In addition, the functionalization of the amine groups on the surface of the PCDs enables PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel also exhibits photoluminescence behavior. The fluorescence intensities of the above hydrogels significantly decreased after adsorbing Fe3+ under UV light and fluorescence spectrophotometer. The results of adsorption isotherms and adsorption kinetics showed that the adsorption procedure of metal ions conforms to the Pseudo-second-order model and Langmuir model, respectively. Due to the effective detection and the high adsorption for Fe3+, the PNIPAAm-CD hydrogel has potential applications as an effective, portable platform with bifunctional of visual detection and adsorption of Fe3+.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Dongdong Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Revise, Calculation model. Xuetao Tian: Validation, Formal analysis. Huanhuan Li: Validation, Formal analysis. Yiping Zhao: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation. Li Chen: Writing - review & editing.

IRT-17R80) and Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (grant number. TD13-5044).
